front: Novel: Clarissa's 'most exquisite moment' was kissing Sally Seton at Bourton; she learns of Septimus Smith's suicide at her party
Novel: a beggar woman sings 'ee um fah um so / foo swee too eem oo' in Regent's Park; a plane spells 'toffee' overhead
Novel: Lily Briscoe finishes her painting; a dinner of Boeuf en Daube declared 'a triumph' by William Bankes in the section 'The Window'
Novel: the section 'Time Passes' reveals in brackets that Prue died in childbirth; a philosopher-father reaches Q but cannot reach R
Novel: a gender-shifting Elizabethan noble lives 300+ years and writes the poem 'The Oak Tree'; inspired by an affair with Vita Sackville-West
Novel: the protagonist burns 57 manuscripts after Nicholas Greene's satirical review, keeping only 'The Oak Tree'
Work: invents Judith Shakespeare, who runs away from marriage, becomes pregnant by Nick Greene, and commits suicide — to illustrate why women need money and a room to write
back: A Room of One's Own (Virginia Woolf)

front: Work: the narrator examines the sentence 'Chloe liked Olivia' from the fictional novel Life's Adventure by Mary Carmichael
back: A Room of One's Own (Virginia Woolf)

front: Novel: a 'play-poem' of nine soliloquies by six friends mourning Percival, who dies in a riding accident in India
Novel: Miss La Trobe directs a history pageant that ends with actors holding mirrors to the audience while a gramophone repeats 'dispersed are we'
Work: the essay 'Modern Fiction' declares 'life is a luminous halo' and attacks H.G. Wells, Galsworthy, and Arnold Bennett as 'materialists'
work: Essays (Modern Fiction, Mr. Bennett and Mrs. Brown, Death of the Moth, On Not Knowing Greek)

front: Work: claims human character changed 'on or about December 1910' in an essay attacking Arnold Bennett's characterization methods
work: Essays (Modern Fiction, Mr. Bennett and Mrs. Brown, Death of the Moth, On Not Knowing Greek)

front: Short Story: alternates between four couples' conversations and a snail struggling past a leaf in a flower bed
Short Story: the narrator muses whether a mark is a nail or a dried rose petal; it turns out to be a snail
Author: participated in the 1910 Dreadnought hoax, dressing as Abyssinian ambassadors with brother Adrian and friends
